rawText:
A startup is a company designed to grow fast. Being newly founded does not in itself make a company a startup. Nor is it necessary for a startup to work on technology, or take venture funding, or have some sort of "exit." The only essential thing is growth. Everything else we associate with startups follows from growth.

If you want to start one it's important to understand that. Startups are so hard that you can't be pointed off to the side and hope to succeed. You have to know that growth is what you're after. The good news is, if you get growth, everything else tends to fall into place. Which means you can use growth like a compass to make almost every decision you face.

Let's start with a distinction that should be obvious but is often overlooked: not every newly founded company is a startup. Millions of companies are started every year in the US. Only a tiny fraction are startups. Most are service businesses — restaurants, barbershops, plumbers, and so on. These are not startups, except in a few unusual cases. A barbershop isn't designed to grow fast. Whereas a search engine, for example, is.

When I say startups are designed to grow fast, I mean it in two senses. Partly I mean designed in the sense of intended, because most startups fail. But I also mean startups are different by nature, in the same way a redwood seedling has a different destiny from a bean sprout.

That difference is why there's a distinct word, "startup," for companies designed to grow fast. If all companies were essentially similar, but some through luck or the efforts of their founders ended up growing very fast, we wouldn't need a separate word. We could just talk about super-successful companies and less successful ones. But in fact startups do have a different sort of DNA from other businesses. Google is not just a barbershop whose founders were unusually lucky and hard-working. Google was different from the beginning.

To grow rapidly, you need to make something you can sell to a big market. That's the difference between Google and a barbershop. A barbershop doesn't scale.

For a company to grow really big, it must (a) make something lots of people want, and (b) reach and serve all those people. Barbershops are doing fine in the (a) department. Almost everyone needs their hair cut. The problem for a barbershop, as for any retail establishment, is (b). A barbershop serves customers in person, and few will travel far for a haircut. And even if they did, the barbershop couldn't accommodate them.

sentences.original: A startup is a company designed to grow fast.
sentences.translation: 스타트업은 빠르게 성장하도록 설계된 회사입니다.

grammarElements.text: a company designed to grow fast
grammarElements.label: 보어 (과거분사구 후치수식)

vocabulary.example: She joined a startup right after college.
This software is designed to simplify accounting.

expressions.usage: 목적이나 의도가 내재된 설계를 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: [A] is [a noun] designed to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 정의를 내릴 때 사용하는 패턴. 과거분사 designed가 명사를 뒤에서 수식.
examples: A bridge is a structure designed to span a gap.
A vaccine is a substance designed to stimulate immunity.

drillQuestions.question: A startup is a company _____ to grow fast.
A startup is designed to grow _____.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 첫 문장이자 핵심 정의. Paul Graham은 스타트업의 본질을 "빠른 성장"이라는 단 하나의 특성으로 압축한다. designed to는 과거분사의 후치수식으로, "~하도록 설계된"이라는 의미.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 정의(definition) — 에세이 전체의 논지를 한 문장으로 압축하는 직접적 정의

sentences.original: Being newly founded does not in itself make a company a startup.
sentences.translation: 새로 설립되었다는 것 자체가 회사를 스타트업으로 만들지는 않습니다.

vocabulary.example: The company was founded in 2005.
Money in itself is not evil.

expressions.usage: 어떤 요소만으로는 충분하지 않음을 강조할 때
사역동사 make의 5형식 구조

patterns.pattern: [Gerund phrase] does not in itself make [A] [B]
patterns.explanation: 어떤 조건이 그 자체로는 충분하지 않음을 주장하는 패턴. 사역동사 make의 5형식 구조.
examples: Having money does not in itself make a person happy.
Being talented does not in itself make someone successful.

drillQuestions.question: Being newly founded does not _____ itself make a company a startup.
Being newly founded does not make a company _____ startup.

sentences.notes: Being newly founded는 동명사구가 주어로 사용된 구조. "in itself"는 "그 자체로"라는 의미의 부사구로 동사(make)를 수식한다. make + 목적어 + 목적격 보어(SVOC) 5형식 구조.
pronunciationNotes: founded: /ˈfaʊndɪd/ — "파운디드", found(발견하다)와 발음 동일하나 의미 다름

sentences.original: Nor is it necessary for a startup to work on technology, or take venture funding, or have some sort of "exit."
sentences.translation: 스타트업이 기술 분야에서 일하거나, 벤처 자금을 받거나, 어떤 종류의 "엑시트"를 가질 필요도 없습니다.

grammarElements.text: to work on technology, or take venture funding, or have some sort of "exit"
grammarElements.label: 진주어 (to부정사 3개 병렬)

vocabulary.example: The startup received $5 million in venture funding.
The founders achieved a successful exit through acquisition.

expressions.usage: 앞의 부정에 이어 추가 부정을 도치로 강조할 때
정확히 규정하지 않고 대략적으로 지칭할 때

patterns.pattern: Nor is it necessary for [someone] to [verb]
patterns.explanation: Nor로 시작하는 도치 구문. 앞의 부정에 이어 "~도 필요 없다"를 강조하는 패턴.
examples: Nor is it necessary for students to memorize every formula.
Nor is it necessary for leaders to be extroverts.

drillQuestions.question: _____ is it necessary for a startup to take venture funding.
Nor is it necessary for a startup to work _____ technology.

sentences.notes: Nor로 시작하면 주어-동사 도치가 일어난다(Nor + is + it). 이는 격식적인 문어체에서 부정을 강조하는 구조. "exit"에 따옴표를 쓴 것은 업계 전문 용어임을 표시한 것.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 도치(inversion) — Nor + 동사 + 주어 구조로 부정 강조를 통해 고정관념 파괴

sentences.original: The only essential thing is growth. Everything else we associate with startups follows from growth.
sentences.translation: 유일하게 필수적인 것은 성장입니다. 우리가 스타트업과 연관짓는 다른 모든 것은 성장에서 비롯됩니다.

grammarElements.text: Everything else we associate with startups
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계대명사 목적격 생략)

vocabulary.example: Water is essential for life.
People often associate success with money.
Several conclusions follow from this analysis.

expressions.usage: 핵심을 극도로 압축하여 강조할 때
논리적 인과관계를 표현할 때. 철학/논증에서 자주 사용

patterns.pattern: The only [adjective] thing is [X]. Everything else follows from [X].
patterns.explanation: 핵심 원리를 제시하고, 나머지가 거기서 파생된다는 것을 주장하는 논증 패턴.
examples: The only important thing is consistency. Everything else follows from consistency.
The only real rule is honesty. Everything else follows from honesty.

drillQuestions.question: The only _____ thing is growth.
Everything else we associate with startups _____ from growth.

sentences.notes: "Everything else we associate with startups"에서 관계대명사(that/which)가 생략되었다. 원래는 "Everything else that we associate with startups". follows from은 논리학 용어로, 어떤 결론이 전제에서 논리적으로 도출됨을 의미한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Everything else we associate with startups

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반복(repetition) + 환원(reduction) — "growth"를 반복하며 모든 것을 하나의 원리로 환원

sentences.original: If you want to start one it's important to understand that.
sentences.translation: 만약 스타트업을 시작하고 싶다면, 그것을 이해하는 것이 중요합니다.

grammarElements.text: If you want to start one
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

vocabulary.example: He decided to start his own business.

expressions.usage: 핵심 메시지를 강조하며 독자의 주의를 환기할 때

patterns.pattern: If you want to [verb], it's important to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 목표를 위한 전제 조건을 제시하는 패턴. 가주어 it + 진주어 to부정사.
examples: If you want to succeed, it's important to set clear goals.
If you want to learn a language, it's important to practice daily.

drillQuestions.question: If you want to start one, _____ important to understand that.
If you want to start one it's important to _____ that.

sentences.notes: "one"은 앞 문장의 "startup"을 받는 대명사. "that"은 앞 단락의 핵심 주장(스타트업 = 성장)을 가리킨다. 비격식적인 문체로, 쉼표 없이 조건절과 주절을 연결한 것이 특징.

koreanAlignment.en: If you want to start one

pronunciationNotes: important: /ɪmˈpɔːrtnt/ — "임포어턴트", t는 거의 묵음 처리되기도 함

sentences.original: Startups are so hard that you can't be pointed off to the side and hope to succeed.
sentences.translation: 스타트업은 너무나 어려워서 옆길로 빠져서는 성공을 기대할 수 없습니다.

grammarElements.text: that you can't be pointed off to the side and hope to succeed
grammarElements.label: 결과절 (so...that)

vocabulary.example: The arrow was pointed off to the left.
She worked hard to succeed in her career.

expressions.usage: so + 형용사 + that 결과절 구조로 정도를 강조

expressions.phrase: be pointed off to the side
expressions.usage: 본래 목표에서 벗어난 상태를 비유적으로 표현

patterns.pattern: [Subject] is so [adjective] that you can't [verb] and hope to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 어떤 것이 너무 어려워서 특정 행동과 기대를 동시에 할 수 없다는 패턴.
examples: The exam is so hard that you can't skip classes and hope to pass.
The competition is so fierce that you can't be lazy and hope to win.

drillQuestions.question: Startups are _____ hard that you can't be distracted.
You can't be pointed off to the side and hope to _____.

sentences.notes: so...that 구문은 정도와 결과를 연결한다. "be pointed off to the side"는 나침반/방향의 비유로, 다음 문단에서 나오는 "compass" 비유의 복선이다. and hope to succeed에서 and는 "그리고서 ~하기를 기대하다"라는 부가적 의미.

koreanAlignment.en: be pointed off to the side

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 비유(metaphor) — 방향과 나침반의 비유로 "집중"의 필요성을 시각화

sentences.original: You have to know that growth is what you're after.
sentences.translation: 성장이 당신이 추구하는 것임을 알아야 합니다.

grammarElements.text: that growth is what you're after
grammarElements.label: that절 (목적어) — what 강조 구문

vocabulary.example: What are you after?

expressions.usage: 목표나 목적을 구어체로 표현할 때
must보다 부드럽지만 강한 의무를 표현

patterns.pattern: You have to know that [X] is what you're after
patterns.explanation: 핵심 목표를 재확인하는 패턴. what 관계대명사절이 보어로 사용되어 강조.
examples: You have to know that quality is what you're after.
You have to know that user satisfaction is what you're after.

drillQuestions.question: You have to know that growth is _____ you're after.
Growth is what you're _____.

sentences.notes: "what you're after"는 what 관계대명사절로, "the thing that you're after"와 같은 의미. be after는 구어체에서 "추구하다"라는 의미로 매우 자주 사용된다. 이 문장은 에세이의 핵심 메시지를 직접적으로 전달한다.

sentences.original: The good news is, if you get growth, everything else tends to fall into place.
sentences.translation: 좋은 소식은, 성장을 이루면 다른 모든 것이 저절로 제자리를 찾는 경향이 있다는 것입니다.

vocabulary.example: People tend to eat more when they are stressed.
After months of planning, everything finally fell into place.

expressions.usage: 긍정적인 정보를 소개할 때
여러 요소가 자연스럽게 맞아떨어질 때

patterns.pattern: The good news is, if you [verb], everything else tends to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 하나의 조건만 충족하면 나머지가 자연스럽게 따라온다는 낙관적 논증.
examples: The good news is, if you exercise regularly, everything else tends to improve.
The good news is, if you master the basics, everything else tends to come naturally.

drillQuestions.question: If you get growth, everything else tends to fall into _____.
Everything else _____ to fall into place.

sentences.notes: fall into place는 퍼즐 조각이 맞아 들어가는 이미지에서 온 숙어. 복잡한 상황이 자연스럽게 해결됨을 의미한다. Paul Graham의 논증 전략: 복잡한 문제를 단순한 원리(성장)로 환원하여 독자에게 명쾌함을 제공.

koreanAlignment.en: everything else tends to fall into place
koreanAlignment.ko: 다른 모든 것이 저절로 제자리를 찾는 경향이 있다는 것입니다

sentences.original: Which means you can use growth like a compass to make almost every decision you face.
sentences.translation: 이는 곧 당신이 직면하는 거의 모든 결정을 내리는 데 나침반처럼 성장을 활용할 수 있다는 뜻입니다.

grammarElements.text: you can use growth like a compass
grammarElements.label: 목적절

grammarElements.text: to make almost every decision you face
grammarElements.label: to부정사 (목적)

vocabulary.example: A compass always points north.
We face many challenges ahead.

expressions.usage: 방향을 제시하는 기준이나 원칙을 비유적으로 표현
앞 내용의 함의를 설명할 때. 문두에서 사용

patterns.pattern: Which means you can use [X] like a [metaphor] to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 앞 문장의 결론을 비유와 함께 실용적 조언으로 전환하는 패턴.
examples: Which means you can use feedback like a mirror to improve yourself.
Which means you can use data like a roadmap to guide your strategy.

drillQuestions.question: You can use growth _____ a compass.
Which means you can use growth to make almost every _____ you face.

sentences.notes: 문두의 Which는 앞 문장 전체("성장을 이루면 다른 것이 따라온다")를 받는 관계대명사. "like a compass"는 에세이의 핵심 비유로, 나중에 "Compass" 섹션의 제목으로 발전한다. 이 비유가 에세이 전체를 관통하는 구조적 메타포.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 비유(metaphor) — "성장 = 나침반"이라는 핵심 비유. 에세이의 구조적 메타포

sentences.original: Let's start with a distinction that should be obvious but is often overlooked: not every newly founded company is a startup.
sentences.translation: 분명해야 하지만 종종 간과되는 구분부터 시작합시다: 모든 새로 설립된 회사가 스타트업인 것은 아닙니다.

grammarElements.text: that should be obvious but is often overlooked
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (주격)

grammarElements.text: not every newly founded company is a startup
grammarElements.label: 콜론 뒤 설명절 (부분 부정)

vocabulary.example: There is an important distinction between the two concepts.
This problem is often overlooked by managers.

expressions.usage: 논증이나 설명을 시작할 때
일반화를 부정하여 예외를 인정할 때

patterns.pattern: Let's start with [a noun] that [should be X] but [is often Y]: [statement]
patterns.explanation: 콜론으로 핵심 주장을 도입하는 에세이 전개 패턴. that절로 구분의 중요성을 부각.
examples: Let's start with a fact that should be well known but is often ignored: most diets don't work.
Let's start with a principle that should be simple but is often misunderstood: less is more.

drillQuestions.question: Let's start with a distinction that should be obvious but is often _____.
_____ every newly founded company is a startup.

sentences.notes: "not every"는 부분 부정(partial negation)으로, "모두 ~인 것은 아니다"라는 의미. "no"(전체 부정)와 구별해야 한다. 콜론(:) 뒤에 핵심 주장을 배치하는 것은 Paul Graham 에세이의 전형적인 스타일.

sentences.original: Millions of companies are started every year in the US. Only a tiny fraction are startups.
sentences.translation: 미국에서 매년 수백만 개의 회사가 창업됩니다. 그중 극소수만이 스타트업입니다.

vocabulary.example: Only a fraction of applicants were accepted.
The difference is tiny but significant.

expressions.usage: 매우 큰 수를 강조할 때
전체에서 매우 작은 비율을 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: Millions of [X] are [verb]. Only a tiny fraction are [Y].
patterns.explanation: 전체의 크기를 먼저 제시한 뒤, 극소수의 특별한 경우를 대비하는 패턴.
examples: Millions of books are published every year. Only a tiny fraction become bestsellers.
Millions of apps are launched annually. Only a tiny fraction survive.

drillQuestions.question: Only a _____ fraction are startups.
Millions of companies are _____ every year.

sentences.notes: "Only a tiny fraction"은 극소수를 강조하는 표현. fraction은 원래 수학의 분수를 의미하지만, 여기서는 "극소수의 비율"이라는 뜻. 수백만과 극소수의 대비가 스타트업의 희소성을 부각.

sentences.original: Most are service businesses — restaurants, barbershops, plumbers, and so on.
sentences.translation: 대부분은 서비스 업종입니다 — 식당, 이발소, 배관공 등등.

grammarElements.text: restaurants, barbershops, plumbers, and so on
grammarElements.label: 동격 (구체적 예시 나열)

vocabulary.example: A restaurant is a typical service business.
I go to the barbershop once a month.
We called a plumber to fix the leak.

expressions.usage: 예시를 나열한 후 더 있음을 암시

patterns.pattern: Most are [category] — [example 1], [example 2], [example 3], and so on.
patterns.explanation: 범주를 제시하고 대시(—) 뒤에 구체적 예시를 나열하는 패턴.
examples: Most are tech companies — social media, e-commerce, cloud services, and so on.
Most are creative fields — music, art, writing, and so on.

drillQuestions.question: Most are service businesses — restaurants, barbershops, plumbers, and _____ on.

sentences.notes: em dash(—)는 구체적 예시를 도입할 때 사용. barbershop은 이후 에세이 전체에서 "스타트업이 아닌 일반 사업"의 대표적 예시로 반복 등장한다.

koreanAlignment.en: restaurants, barbershops, plumbers, and so on

sentences.original: A barbershop isn't designed to grow fast. Whereas a search engine, for example, is.
sentences.translation: 이발소는 빠르게 성장하도록 설계되지 않았습니다. 반면에 검색 엔진은 예를 들어 그렇습니다.

grammarElements.label: 동사 (생략된 보어: designed to grow fast)

vocabulary.example: He likes coffee, whereas she prefers tea.
Google is the most popular search engine.

expressions.usage: 두 가지를 극적으로 대비할 때. while보다 격식적
구체적 예시를 삽입할 때

patterns.pattern: [A] isn't designed to [verb]. Whereas [B], for example, is.
patterns.explanation: 두 대상의 본질적 차이를 대비하는 패턴. 두 번째 문장에서 반복을 피해 생략(ellipsis) 사용.
examples: A local shop isn't designed to go global. Whereas an online platform, for example, is.
A textbook isn't designed to entertain. Whereas a podcast, for example, is.

drillQuestions.question: A barbershop isn't _____ to grow fast.
_____ a search engine, for example, is.

sentences.notes: 두 번째 문장 "Whereas a search engine, for example, is."에서 "is" 뒤의 "designed to grow fast"가 생략(ellipsis)되어 있다. 이는 반복을 피하는 세련된 문체. barbershop(이발소)과 search engine(검색 엔진)의 대비는 에세이 전체에서 반복되는 핵심 대비 구도.

koreanAlignment.en: A barbershop isn't designed to grow fast

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 대비(contrast) + 생략(ellipsis) — 이발소와 검색 엔진의 대조를 생략법으로 간결하게 표현

sentences.original: When I say startups are designed to grow fast, I mean it in two senses.
sentences.translation: 스타트업이 빠르게 성장하도록 설계되었다고 말할 때, 저는 두 가지 의미로 말하는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: startups are designed to grow fast
grammarElements.label: 목적절 (say의 내용)

vocabulary.example: The word has several different senses.
What do you mean by that?

expressions.phrase: when I say X, I mean it in
expressions.meaning: X라고 말할 때, ~의 의미로 말하는 것이다
expressions.usage: 자신의 표현을 정교하게 설명할 때
하나의 표현에 여러 층위의 의미가 있을 때

patterns.pattern: When I say [X], I mean it in [number] senses.
patterns.explanation: 자신의 주장을 여러 의미 층위로 분리하여 설명하는 메타 논증 패턴.
examples: When I say education matters, I mean it in two senses.
When I say design is important, I mean it in three senses.

drillQuestions.question: When I say startups are designed to grow fast, I _____ it in two senses.
I mean it in two _____.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 메타 담론(meta-discourse)의 예로, 저자가 자신의 표현("designed")을 독자에게 해명하는 구조. Paul Graham은 종종 하나의 단어를 여러 의미로 분석하여 논증의 깊이를 더한다.

koreanAlignment.en: startups are designed to grow fast

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 메타 담론(meta-discourse) — 자신의 말을 독자에게 해명하며 논증을 전개

sentences.original: Partly I mean designed in the sense of intended, because most startups fail.
sentences.translation: 부분적으로는 designed를 "의도된"이라는 의미로 말합니다. 대부분의 스타트업이 실패하기 때문입니다.

grammarElements.text: designed in the sense of intended
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (의미 설명)

vocabulary.example: The success was partly due to hard work.
The message had its intended effect.

expressions.usage: 단어의 특정 의미를 명확히 할 때
여러 이유 중 하나를 설명할 때

patterns.pattern: Partly I mean [X] in the sense of [Y], because [reason]
patterns.explanation: 단어의 의미를 정밀하게 분석하고 그 이유를 설명하는 학술적 논증 패턴.
examples: Partly I mean freedom in the sense of autonomy, because true freedom requires self-governance.

drillQuestions.question: I mean designed in the _____ of intended.
_____ most startups fail, designed means intended.

sentences.notes: "designed in the sense of intended"는 "설계된"이 아니라 "의도된"이라는 뜻이라는 설명. 스타트업은 빠른 성장을 "의도"하지만 대부분 실패하므로, 실제로 빠르게 "성장하는" 것이 아니라 빠른 성장을 "목표로 하는" 것이라는 미묘한 의미 구분.

koreanAlignment.en: designed in the sense of intended

sentences.original: But I also mean startups are different by nature, in the same way a redwood seedling has a different destiny from a bean sprout.
sentences.translation: 하지만 스타트업은 본질적으로 다르다는 의미이기도 합니다. 레드우드 묘목이 콩나물과 다른 운명을 가진 것과 같은 방식으로.

grammarElements.text: a redwood seedling has a different destiny from a bean sprout
grammarElements.label: 비교절

vocabulary.example: He is generous by nature.
The seedlings need plenty of sunlight.
She believed it was her destiny to become a doctor.
Bean sprouts are a popular ingredient in Asian cooking.

expressions.usage: 유사성을 비유로 설명할 때
표면적 유사성 너머의 근본적 차이를 강조할 때

patterns.pattern: [X] is different by nature, in the same way [A] has a different [noun] from [B]
patterns.explanation: 본질적 차이를 자연의 비유로 설명하는 패턴.
examples: A novel is different by nature, in the same way a river has a different course from a pond.
An entrepreneur is different by nature, in the same way an eagle has a different flight from a sparrow.

drillQuestions.question: Startups are different _____ nature.
A redwood seedling has a different destiny _____ a bean sprout.

sentences.notes: 레드우드(세쿼이아) 묘목과 콩나물의 비유는 에세이에서 가장 기억에 남는 비유 중 하나. 둘 다 작은 식물이지만, 하나는 100미터 거목이 될 "운명"을 가지고 있다. 이 비유가 "Redwoods" 섹션 제목의 이유. different from이 different than보다 격식적.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 유비(analogy) — 레드우드 묘목 vs 콩나물 비유로 스타트업의 본질적 차이를 시각화

sentences.original: That difference is why there's a distinct word, "startup," for companies designed to grow fast.
sentences.translation: 그 차이가 바로 빠르게 성장하도록 설계된 회사들을 위해 "스타트업"이라는 별도의 단어가 존재하는 이유입니다.

grammarElements.text: for companies designed to grow fast
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (대상)

vocabulary.example: There are three distinct categories.

expressions.usage: 원인과 결과를 연결하여 설명할 때

patterns.pattern: That [noun] is why there's a [adjective] [noun] for [X]
patterns.explanation: 어떤 차이나 특성이 별도의 개념/단어가 존재하는 이유임을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: That complexity is why there's a specialized field for it.
That uniqueness is why there's a separate term for it.

drillQuestions.question: That difference is _____ there's a distinct word.
There's a _____ word, "startup," for these companies.

sentences.notes: "That difference"는 앞 문단의 레드우드/콩나물 비유에서 확립된 본질적 차이를 가리킨다. "startup"에 따옴표를 사용하여 단어 자체를 언급(use-mention distinction)하고 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: for companies designed to grow fast

sentences.original: But in fact startups do have a different sort of DNA from other businesses.
sentences.translation: 하지만 실제로 스타트업은 다른 사업체와 다른 종류의 DNA를 가지고 있습니다.

vocabulary.example: He seemed calm, but in fact he was very nervous.
Innovation is in the company's DNA.

expressions.usage: 일반동사 앞에 do를 넣어 긍정을 강조하는 구문
kind of, type of보다 약간 격식적인 분류 표현

patterns.pattern: But in fact [subject] do have a different [noun] from [Y]
patterns.explanation: 일반적 인식과 달리 실제로 차이가 있음을 do 강조 구문으로 주장하는 패턴.
examples: But in fact experts do have a different perspective from beginners.
But in fact leaders do have a different mindset from followers.

drillQuestions.question: Startups _____ have a different sort of DNA.
Startups have a different sort of DNA _____ other businesses.

sentences.notes: "do have"에서 do는 강조의 조동사(emphatic do). 앞 문장들이 가정("If all companies were essentially similar...")을 세우고, 이 문장이 "But in fact"으로 현실을 반박하는 구조. DNA는 생물학 용어를 비유적으로 사용하여 스타트업의 본질적 특성을 표현.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 강조 do(emphatic do) + 생물학적 비유(DNA) — 강조 구문과 비유를 결합하여 본질적 차이를 역설

sentences.original: Google is not just a barbershop whose founders were unusually lucky and hard-working. Google was different from the beginning.
sentences.translation: Google은 단지 창업자들이 유난히 운이 좋고 열심히 일한 이발소가 아닙니다. Google은 처음부터 달랐습니다.

grammarElements.text: whose founders were unusually lucky and hard-working
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (whose — 소유격)

grammarElements.text: Google was different from the beginning
grammarElements.label: 독립절

vocabulary.example: The weather was unusually warm this winter.
She is one of the most hard-working people I know.

expressions.usage: 축소 해석을 반박할 때
본질적이고 근원적인 차이를 강조할 때

patterns.pattern: [X] is not just [a lesser version]. [X] was different from the beginning.
patterns.explanation: 대상이 단순히 성공한 평범한 것이 아니라 본질적으로 다름을 주장하는 패턴.
examples: Amazon is not just a bookstore that got big. Amazon was different from the beginning.
Tesla is not just a car company that went electric. Tesla was different from the beginning.

drillQuestions.question: Google is not _____ a barbershop whose founders were lucky.
Google was different _____ the beginning.

sentences.notes: whose는 소유격 관계대명사로 "그 이발소의 창업자들이"라는 의미. Google과 이발소의 대비는 의도적으로 터무니없어 보이는 비교를 통해 논점을 극적으로 만든다. "from the beginning"은 성공이 운이 아닌 본질적 차이에서 온다는 핵심 주장.

koreanAlignment.ko: 유난히 운이 좋고 열심히 일한 이발소가

koreanAlignment.en: Google was different from the beginning

pronunciationNotes: whose: /huːz/ — "후즈", who's(who is)와 발음 동일하나 의미 다름
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반어적 대비(ironic contrast) — Google과 이발소의 터무니없는 비교로 논점을 극대화

sentences.original: To grow rapidly, you need to make something you can sell to a big market. That's the difference between Google and a barbershop. A barbershop doesn't scale.
sentences.translation: 빠르게 성장하려면, 큰 시장에 팔 수 있는 것을 만들어야 합니다. 그것이 Google과 이발소의 차이입니다. 이발소는 확장되지 않습니다.

grammarElements.text: something you can sell to a big market
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (관계대명사 목적격 생략)

grammarElements.text: That's the difference between Google and a barbershop
grammarElements.label: 독립절

vocabulary.example: Technology is changing rapidly.
Can this business scale?
The company is targeting the Asian market.

expressions.usage: 두 대상의 핵심 차이를 명쾌하게 요약할 때
비즈니스가 규모 성장에 한계가 있을 때. 실리콘밸리 용어

patterns.pattern: To [verb], you need to [verb]. That's the difference between [X] and [Y]. [Y] doesn't [verb].
patterns.explanation: 목표 달성의 조건을 제시하고, 두 대상의 차이를 간결하게 정리하는 3단 논증 패턴.
examples: To win globally, you need to think beyond borders. That's the difference between a local brand and a global one. A local brand doesn't expand.

drillQuestions.question: You need to make something you can sell to a big _____.
A barbershop doesn't ___.

sentences.notes: "scale"은 실리콘밸리에서 가장 중요한 개념 중 하나. "확장 가능하다"는 의미로, 추가 비용 없이 더 많은 고객을 서비스할 수 있는 능력을 말한다. "A barbershop doesn't scale."는 네 단어로 핵심을 압축한 명문. "something you can sell"에서 관계대명사(that)가 생략되었다.

koreanAlignment.en: you can sell to a big market
That's the difference between Google and a barbershop

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 간결한 결론(brevity) — "A barbershop doesn't scale." 네 단어로 핵심 차이를 압축
